comment.body:
I've figured for a bit that one path is that things get destroyed quicker than they can be rebuilt.  In some key areas, at least in the US, major damage to things like power infrastructure could not be repaired quickly.

And in the "you own nothing and you will like it" dept, very high insurance premiums could make owning houses etc in some areas impossible since no bank will give you a loan without it.

comment.body:
If the print is “positive demand-side shock”-ish (GDP up/prices up),
the initial reaction will also be pessimistic as market re-prices its Fed cut odds. But if data is not hot, it also can get a benign read on the short-run too.

comment.body:
oks soooo....
https://www.bea.gov/news/2025/gross-domestic-product-3rd-quarter-2025-initial-estimate-and-corporate-profits

growth materially above expectations with inflation also higher than the prior quarter inside the same release.
this looks more like a positive demand impulse with some supply/trade distortions (import compression + inventory drawdown), so be careful with supply improvement narratives.
